summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that an unauthenticated attacker can read arbitrary files on the FUXA server via path traversal in GET /api/logs or GET /api/download. The single-pass ../ regex removal is bypassed using ....// sequences that reconstruct to ../ after the replacement.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction pipeline should cause the path traversal payload to bypass the single-pass regex sanitization and return the contents of /etc/passwd from the server.
actual_observed_result: The exploit sends GET /api/logs?file=....//....//....//....//etc/passwd. The single-pass regex removes the embedded ../ but the remaining characters reconstruct to a valid traversal path. If the /api/logs endpoint returns an error, the exploit falls back to GET /api/download?cmd=reportDownload&name=....//....//....//....//etc/passwd.
verification_method: The exploit checks whether the HTTP response body contains the string 'root:' which is the standard first-field entry in /etc/passwd. This distinguishes successful file read from error messages, empty responses, or application-level access denied messages.
evidence_produced_by_run: The GET /api/logs endpoint accepted the crafted filename parameter with the bypass sequence.
The regex replacement was bypassed as predicted: ....// -> ../ after single-pass removal.
No authentication token was required because secureEnabled defaults to false.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh downloads the target repository archive from GitHub, checks out the pinned vulnerable commit, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
